roadmap.title: Learn Portuguese for Beginners: A Step-by-Step Guide

modules.title: Foundations of Pronunciation
modules.focus: Master the sounds of the Portuguese alphabet, including nasal vowels and unique consonant pronunciations.
objectives: Identify and pronounce all vowels, including open, closed, and nasal sounds
Read basic words aloud with correct syllable stress and intonation
Understand the primary pronunciation differences between Brazilian and European Portuguese
modules.description: Master the sounds of the Portuguese alphabet, including nasal vowels and unique consonant pronunciations.

modules.title: Greetings and Introductions
modules.focus: Learn essential phrases for greeting people, introducing yourself, and showing basic courtesy.
objectives: Use appropriate greetings based on the time of day and formality level
Introduce yourself by stating your name, nationality, and origin
Employ basic polite expressions such as please, thank you, and excuse me
modules.description: Learn essential phrases for greeting people, introducing yourself, and showing basic courtesy.

modules.title: Nouns, Articles, and Gender
modules.focus: Understand how Portuguese nouns are categorized by gender and how to use definite and indefinite articles.
objectives: Determine the gender of nouns based on their word endings
Match definite and indefinite articles to the gender and number of nouns
Form basic plural nouns by applying standard spelling rules
modules.description: Understand how Portuguese nouns are categorized by gender and how to use definite and indefinite articles.

modules.title: Subject Pronouns and the Verb 'To Be'
modules.focus: Learn subject pronouns and the distinctions between the two verbs for 'to be' (ser and estar).
objectives: Identify subject pronouns in Portuguese (eu, tu, ele, ela, nós, etc.)
Conjugate the verb 'ser' to express permanent characteristics and identities
Conjugate the verb 'estar' to express temporary states, emotions, and locations
modules.description: Learn subject pronouns and the distinctions between the two verbs for 'to be' (ser and estar).

modules.title: Regular Verbs in the Present Tense
modules.focus: Master the conjugation patterns for regular -AR, -ER, and -IR verbs in the present tense.
objectives: Recognize the infinitive form of verbs and identify their conjugation group
Conjugate regular -AR verbs to describe daily actions
Conjugate regular -ER and -IR verbs to build basic conversational sentences
modules.description: Master the conjugation patterns for regular -AR, -ER, and -IR verbs in the present tense.

modules.title: Numbers, Days, and Time
modules.focus: Learn how to count, use the calendar, and tell time in Portuguese.
objectives: Count from 0 to 100 and understand larger number patterns
Name the days of the week, months of the year, and seasons
Ask for and state the time of day accurately
modules.description: Learn how to count, use the calendar, and tell time in Portuguese.

modules.title: Asking Questions and Negation
modules.focus: Learn how to form questions using interrogative words and how to make sentences negative.
objectives: Use question words like 'o que', 'onde', 'quando', 'por que', and 'quem'
Form yes/no questions by adjusting intonation or adding tags
Apply negative words such as 'não' and 'nunca' to sentences
modules.description: Learn how to form questions using interrogative words and how to make sentences negative.

modules.title: Describing Things: Adjectives and Possessives
modules.focus: Learn how to describe nouns using adjectives and indicate ownership with possessive pronouns.
objectives: Ensure adjectives agree with the nouns they modify in gender and number
Use common descriptive adjectives for size, color, and age
Employ possessive pronouns to indicate ownership of objects and relationships
modules.description: Learn how to describe nouns using adjectives and indicate ownership with possessive pronouns.

modules.title: Essential Vocabulary for Daily Life
modules.focus: Build a practical vocabulary base covering family, food, colors, and common objects.
objectives: Name common family members and relationships
Identify basic food and drink items found in restaurants and markets
Use color adjectives to describe everyday objects
modules.description: Build a practical vocabulary base covering family, food, colors, and common objects.

modules.title: Expressing Wants, Needs, and the Future
modules.focus: Learn modal verbs and the immediate future tense to express intentions and upcoming actions.
objectives: Conjugate 'querer' (to want) and 'precisar' (to need) in the present tense
Use the verb 'ir' to form the immediate future tense
Combine modal verbs with infinitives to express desires and obligations
modules.description: Learn modal verbs and the immediate future tense to express intentions and upcoming actions.

modules.title: Navigating Everyday Situations
modules.focus: Apply your knowledge to practical scenarios like ordering food, asking for directions, and shopping.
objectives: Order food and drinks confidently at a restaurant or cafe
Ask for and understand basic directions to navigate a city
Interact with shop clerks to ask for prices and purchase items

modules.title: Foundations of Pronunciation
modules.content:
## Why Portuguese Sounds So Different Than It Looks

Imagine you are sitting at a café in Lisbon or São Paulo. You learned a few basic words from an app before your trip—words like *pão* (bread) and *sim* (yes). You felt confident. But when the locals speak, the words sound completely different than you expected. The vowels seem to shift and melt into one another, certain consonants sound like they are being whispered, and some words have a distinct hum vibrating through the nose. 

This is the Portuguese sound puzzle. Portuguese is a beautifully phonetic language, but its spelling does not always tell the whole story of how it should be spoken. A single letter can make multiple sounds depending on where it sits in a word, and the way words are chopped into beats (syllables) dictates which part gets the emphasis.

Before you learn to string sentences together, you need to build the machinery in your mouth to produce these sounds. By mastering the alphabet, the five vowel personalities, the nasal hum, and the unique consonants, you will build a foundation that makes every future word you learn easier to say and understand.

## The Portuguese Alphabet: A Quick Tour

The Portuguese alphabet uses the same 26 Latin letters as the English alphabet. For a long time, the letters **K**, **W**, and **Y** were not officially part of the Portuguese alphabet because they only appeared in foreign loanwords. However, under the 2009 Portuguese Language Orthographic Agreement, they were officially incorporated. 

While the letters are the same, the *names* of the letters are different. More importantly, the sounds they make are distinct. 

A helpful concept to grasp before diving into specific letters is the idea of **open** and **closed** vowels. 
*   An **open vowel** is pronounced with your mouth wider open and your tongue lower. It sounds brighter and louder. Think of the "a" in the English word "cat."
*   A **closed vowel** is pronounced with your mouth more relaxed and slightly closed. It sounds softer and deeper. Think of the "o" in the English word "cold."

## The Five Vowel Personalities

In English, vowels are notoriously unpredictable. In Portuguese, vowels are much more consistent, but a single letter can still make two distinct sounds: an open sound or a closed sound. 

Let’s look at the five basic vowels: **A, E, I, O, U**.

### The Letter A
*   **Open A:** Sounds like the "a" in "father." This happens in most stressed syllables.
    *   *Example:* **p**a**la**vra (word) — pronounced pah-LAH-vrah.
*   **Closed A:** Sounds like the "u" in "cup" or the "e" in "above." This happens in unstressed syllables at the end of a word.
    *   *Example:* f**a**l**a** (he/she speaks) — pronounced FAH-luh. 

### The Letter E
*   **Open E:** Sounds like the "e" in "pet." 
    *   *Example:* **e**l**e** (he) — pronounced EH-lee.
*   **Closed E:** Sounds like the "ay" in "say," but shorter and without the trailing "y" sound.
    *   *Example:* v**o**c**ê** (you) — pronounced voh-SAY. *(Note: In Brazilian Portuguese, a final "e" often sounds exactly like an "i". We will explore this regional difference shortly).*

### The Letter I
The letter **I** is highly consistent. It sounds like the "ee" in "see" or the "i" in "machine."
*   *Example:* s**i**m (yes) — pronounced seem.

### The Letter O
*   **Open O:** Sounds like the "o" in "pot."
    *   *Example:* **o**l**o** (eye) — pronounced AW-loo.
*   **Closed O:** Sounds like the "o" in "go," but shorter.
    *   *Example:* **o**s s**o**l (the sun) — pronounced oo SOHL.

### The Letter U
The letter **U** is also highly consistent. It sounds like the "oo" in "food" or the "u" in "flute."
*   *Example:* **u**m (one/a) — pronounced oong.

## The Nasal Secret: Humming Your Vowels

If there is one feature that defines the Portuguese accent, it is **nasalization**. 

A **nasal vowel** is created when air escapes through both your mouth and your nose simultaneously. If you hold your finger lightly against the side of your nose and say the English word "can't," you will feel your nose vibrate on the "a." That is a nasal sound. 

In Portuguese, you do not nasalize vowels just for emphasis; it is a required phonetic rule triggered by specific letters. 

### How Nasalization Works
Nasal sounds are usually triggered by the letter **M**, the letter **N**, or a tilde (**~**). 

**1. M and N at the end of a syllable**
When an **M** or **N** appears at the end of a syllable, the preceding vowel becomes nasal, and the M or N itself is barely pronounced. You do not close your lips for the M, nor do you touch your tongue to the roof of your mouth for the N. You simply hum the vowel.
*   *Example:* **pão** (bread). Instead of saying "pow-n," you say "pão" — the "a" is hummed through the nose, and the tongue simply stops without making a hard "n" sound. 
*   *Example:* **sim** (yes). Instead of "seem," you say "see" through your nose, letting the air trail off without pressing your lips together.

**2. The Tilde (~)**
The tilde explicitly tells you to nasalize the vowel. It only appears over **A** and **O**.
*   *Example:* **mãe** (mother) — pronounced roughly like "mane" but entirely through the nose.
*   *Example:* **limões** (lemons) — the "o" is nasalized, sounding like "lee-moynsh."

## Unique Consonant Pronunciations

Most Portuguese consonants sound similar to their English counterparts, but a few behave very differently. Mastering these will instantly make you sound more like a native speaker.

### The Soft C and S (The "Hissing" Sound)
When the letter **C** has a cedilla (**Ç**), or when **C** and **S** appear before the letters **E** or **I**, they make a soft "hissing" sound.
*   **Ç:** Sounds like the "s" in "sun." 
    *   *Example:* **coração** (heart) — pronounced koh-rah-SOWN.
*   **C before E/I:** Also sounds like "s."
    *   *Example:* **cidade** (city) — pronounced see-DAH-jee.
*   **S before E/I:** Also sounds like "s."
    *   *Example:* **sinta** (feel) — pronounced SEEN-tah.

### The Letter R (The Throaty Roll)
The letter **R** in Portuguese is a chameleon. Its sound depends entirely on where it is in the word.
*   **Double R (RR) or R at the beginning of a word:** This is a guttural sound produced in the back of the throat. Depending on the region, it sounds like the French "r", the "ch" in the Scottish "loch," or a soft "h" in English.
    *   *Example:* **cachorro** (dog) — pronounced kah-SHOH-hoo.
    *   *Example:* **rápido** (fast) — pronounced HAH-pee-doo.
*   **Single R in the middle of a word:** This sounds like the flap "t" in the American English pronunciation of "water" or the "dd" in "ladder." It is a quick tap of the tongue against the roof of the mouth.
    *   *Example:* **caro** (expensive) — pronounced KAH-roo.

### The Letter L (The "W" Sound)
When **L** appears at the end of a syllable or word, it does not sound like the English "L." Instead, it turns into a "w" sound. 
*   *Example:* **Brasil** (Brazil) — pronounced brah-ZEEW.
*   *Example:* **alto** (tall) — pronounced AW-too.

### The Letter D and T (Before I and E)
In Brazilian Portuguese, when **D** comes before an **I** or an **E**, it often sounds like the "j" in "judge." When **T** comes before an **I** or an **E**, it sounds like the "ch" in "church."
*   *Example:* **dia** (day) — pronounced JEE-ah.
*   *Example:* **noite** (night) — pronounced NOY-chee.

## Syllable Stress: Finding the Rhythm

Portuguese has a highly predictable rhythm. Every word is broken into **syllables** (beats of sound), and one of those beats always gets more emphasis—this is called **syllable stress**. 

If you stress the wrong syllable, native speakers might not understand you, or you might accidentally say a completely different word. 

Here are the three golden rules for syllable stress in Portuguese:

**1. The Acute Accent Rule**
If a word has an acute accent (**á**, **é**, **ó**) or a circumflex (**â**, **ê**, **ô**), the stressed syllable is exactly where the accent is. No exceptions.
*   *Example:* **médico** (doctor). The accent is on the first syllable, so you stress it: MEH-dee-koo.
*   *Example:* **jornal** (newspaper). The accent is on the last syllable: zhoor-NAHL.

**2. The Final Letter Rules (For words without accents)**
If a word does not have an accent, the stress is determined by the final letter:
*   **Ends in L, Z, R, M, or N:** Stress the **last** syllable.
    *   *Example:* **falar** (to speak) — fah-LAHR.
    *   *Example:* **tambem** (also) — tahm-BAYNG.
*   **Ends in any other letter (most commonly A, O, E):** Stress the **second-to-last** syllable.
    *   *Example:* **casa** (house) — KAH-zuh.
    *   *Example:* **gato** (cat) — GAH-too.

**3. Diphthongs**
A **diphthong** is when two vowels appear in the same syllable and blend into a single sound (like the "ow" in "cow" or the "ay" in "play"). Portuguese has several diphthongs, such as **ai** (sounds like "eye"), **au** (sounds like "ow"), and **ei** (sounds like "ay"). 
*   *Example:* **pai** (father) — pronounced like the English word "pie."
*   *Example:* **leite** (milk) — pronounced LAY-chee.

## Brazilian vs. European Portuguese: The Great Divide

If you listen to a speaker from São Paulo and a speaker from Lisbon, you might think they are speaking entirely different languages. While the grammar and spelling are largely the same (thanks to the 2009 Orthographic Agreement), the pronunciation and intonation are vastly different. 

As a beginner, you do not need to choose one immediately, but understanding the primary differences will help you avoid confusion when listening to native audio.

### Vowel Reduction
European Portuguese (EP) heavily reduces unstressed vowels. Speakers often drop the sound of vowels entirely, swallowing them. 
*   In Brazilian Portuguese (BP), the word *telefone* (telephone) is pronounced clearly: teh-LEH-foh-nee.
*   In EP, it sounds much shorter and tighter, often dropping the final "e" entirely: teh-LEF-n. 

### The Letter S at the End of Words
How someone pronounces a final "S" is the quickest way to identify their accent.
*   **Brazilian Portuguese:** A final "s" sounds like a normal "s" (as in "sun") or a "z" (as in "zoo") if the next word starts with a vowel.
*   **European Portuguese:** A final "s" (and a final "z") is pronounced like the "sh" in "shoe." 
    *   *Example:* **os dois** (the two). A Brazilian says "oos dohz." A Portuguese person says "oosh doish."

### The Letter D and T
As mentioned earlier, Brazilians turn D and T into "J" and "CH" sounds before I and E. 
*   European speakers do not do this. They keep the sounds crisp. 
*   *Example:* **devagar** (slowly). A Brazilian says "jee-vah-GAHR." A European says "deh-vah-GAHR."

### Intonation
Brazilian Portuguese is famous for its musical, melodic intonation. Sentences often rise and fall in pitch, and words are spoken at a relatively steady pace. European Portuguese is spoken faster, with a staccato rhythm and a flatter intonation, which can make it sound clipped or abrupt to untrained ears.

## Putting It Into Practice: A Pronunciation Scenario

Let’s look at a simple phrase and break down how to pronounce it using the rules we have covered. 

**Phrase:** *Eu como um pão.* (I eat a bread/a roll.)

**1. Break into syllables and find the stress:**
*   **Eu** (I): One syllable. No accent, ends in U, so it is stressed by default.
*   **co-mo** (eat): Two syllables. No accent, ends in O, so stress the second-to-last: CO-mo.
*   **um** (one/a): One syllable.
*   **pão** (bread): One syllable. Has a tilde, so it is stressed and nasal.

**2. Apply the consonant and vowel rules:**
*   **Eu:** The "e" is a closed "e" (like "ay" but shorter), and the "u" is "oo." Sounds like "ay-oo." (In EP, this often compresses to just "ehw").
*   **co-mo:** The "o" sounds like the "o" in "go" (shortened). Sounds like "KOH-moo."
*   **um:** The "u" is "oo" and the "m" nasalizes it. Do not close your lips. Let it hum. Sounds like "oong."
*   **pão:** The "a" is an open "ah," and the tilde makes it nasal. Sounds like "powng" but entirely through the nose.

**3. Put it together:**
*   Brazilian: *Ay-oo KOH-moo oong powng.*
*   European: *Ehw KOH-moo oong powng.*

## Key Takeaways

*   **Open vs. Closed Vowels:** Vowels in Portuguese change their sound based on stress. Open vowels are loud and bright (mouth wider), while closed vowels are soft and deep (mouth relaxed).
*   **Nasalization is Mandatory:** When an **M**, **N**, or tilde (**~**) follows a vowel, you must hum that vowel through your nose. Do not fully pronounce the M or N if it ends a syllable.
*   **Consonant Quirks:** Final **L**s sound like "W"s. Double **R**s and initial **R**s are guttural (from the throat). In Brazilian Portuguese, **D** before I/E sounds like "J," and **T** before I/E sounds like "CH."
*   **Syllable Stress Rules:** If there is an accent mark, stress that syllable. If there is no accent, stress the last syllable if the word ends in L, Z, R, M, or N. Otherwise, stress the second-to-last syllable.
*   **Regional Differences:** Brazilian Portuguese is melodic, clearly pronounces vowels, and turns final S's into "s/z" sounds. European Portuguese is faster, swallows unstressed vowels, and turns final S's into "sh" sounds.

modules.title: Greetings and Introductions
modules.content:
## Greetings Based on the Time of Day

Imagine you are walking into a small bakery in Lisbon on a Tuesday morning. The warm smell of baked bread fills the air, and the person behind the counter looks up, smiles, and says, *"Bom dia!"* 

In Portuguese, greetings are deeply tied to the time of day. Unlike English, where "Hello" or "Hi" can be used at any hour, Portuguese speakers switch their basic greeting depending on whether it is morning, afternoon, or evening. 

Here are the three essential time-based greetings:

*   **Bom dia**: Good morning / Good day
*   **Boa tarde**: Good afternoon
*   **Boa noite**: Good evening / Good night

Notice the words **bom** and **boa**. Both words translate to "good." As you will discover in later chapters, Portuguese words change their endings to match the things they describe. For now, just memorize them as fixed phrases: *bom* goes with *dia*, and *boa* goes with both *tarde* and *noite*.

### Pronunciation Notes
Let’s apply the rules from the **Foundations of Pronunciation** chapter to these new phrases:

*   **Bom dia**: The **o** in *bom* is a **Closed O:**. This means it sounds much like the "oo" in the English word "book." The **m** at the end of *bom* creates a **nasal vowel**. You do not fully close your lips to make a hard "m" sound; instead, you let the sound resonate through your nose. The **i** in *dia* is an **open vowel**, sounding like the "ee" in "see."
*   **Boa tarde**: The **o** in *boa* is a **Closed O:**. The **a** sounds is an **Open A:**, similar to the "a" in "father." In *tarde*, the final **e** is a **Closed E:**. Because it is at the end of the word, it sounds like the "ee" in "see" (in Brazil) or a muted, slightly swallowed sound (in European Portuguese).
*   **Boa noite**: The **oi** in *noite* is a diphthong (two vowel sounds combined). The **o** starts as an **Open O:** and glides into the **i** (**open vowel**). 

### When to Use Which Greeting
The transitions between these greetings are based loosely on meal times rather than exact clock hours:

1.  **Bom dia**: Use from the time you wake up until around 12:00 PM (noon).
2.  **Boa tarde**: Use from noon until the sun goes down (usually around 6:00 PM or 7:00 PM, depending on the season).
3.  **Boa noite**: Use once it is dark outside. Note that *Boa noite* is used both as a greeting when you meet someone in the evening, and as a farewell when you are leaving for the night.

## Informal vs. Formal Greetings

Time-based greetings are your safest bet in any situation. However, Portuguese also distinguishes between informal and formal contexts. 

If you are greeting a friend, a peer, or a child, you can use informal greetings. If you are greeting an elder, a boss, or someone you do not know well, it is polite to use formal greetings.

### The Informal "Hi"
The most common informal greeting is:
*   **Olá**: Hello / Hi

**Olá** is universally understood and perfectly acceptable in most casual situations. The first **o** is an **Open O:**, and the **á** has an accent mark, making it an **Open A:**. 

Another very common informal greeting, especially in Brazil, is:
*   **Oi**: Hi

**Oi** is extremely casual. The **o** is an **Open O:**, and the **i** is an **open vowel** (like "ee"). 

### The Formal Greeting
If you want to show respect, particularly to an older person or in a professional setting, you can say:
*   **Senhor**: Sir
*   **Senhora**: Ma'am / Mrs.

You will often pair these directly with a time-based greeting. For example, walking into a formal office in the afternoon, you might say: *"Boa tarde, senhor."* (Good afternoon, sir.)

The **nh** in *senhora* produces a unique sound. It sounds like the "ny" in the English word "canyon." The final **a** is an **Open A:**.

## Saying Goodbye

Just as you greet people based on the time of day, you also have specific phrases for saying goodbye. 

*   **Adeus**: Goodbye (Formal / Final)
*   **Tchau**: Bye (Informal / Universal)

**Tchau** (derived from the Italian *ciao*) is the most common way to say goodbye in both Brazil and Portugal. It is used in almost all casual settings. The "ch" sounds exactly like the "ch" in the English word "chair." 

**Adeus** carries a slightly heavier weight. It translates literally to "to God." While it means "goodbye," it is often used when you do not expect to see the person for a long time, or in very formal situations. For everyday departures, stick to **tchau**.

If you want to say "See you later," you can use:
*   **Até logo**: See you later. (The **o** in *logo* is a **Closed O:**).
*   **Até amanhã**: See you tomorrow. (The **ã** in *amanhã** uses a tilde (~), creating a **nasal vowel**).

## Introducing Yourself

Now that you know how to greet someone, the next step is telling them who you are. To do this, you only need one simple phrase:

*   **Eu sou...**: I am...

The word **Eu** means "I." The word **sou** means "am." 

### Pronunciation Note
The **E** in *Eu* is a **Closed E:**, and the **u** is a **closed vowel** (like the "oo" in "book"). When said together, *Eu* sounds somewhat like "eh-oo." The **ou** in *sou* creates a distinct sound that glides from an **Open O:** to a **Closed U:**.

To introduce yourself, simply attach your name to the end of the phrase.

**Scenario:**
You are at a coffee shop, and the person next to you strikes up a conversation. 
> **Them:** *Olá! Tudo bem?* (Hi! Is everything good?)
> **You:** *Olá! Eu sou [Your Name].* (Hi! I am [Your Name].)

You do not need to worry about learning the word for "my name is" at this stage. Using *"Eu sou..."* is perfectly natural, highly common, and gets the job done efficiently for a beginner.

## Stating Your Nationality and Origin

Once you have stated your name, the person you are speaking to will often want to know where you are from. You can share this information by adding a few more words to *"Eu sou..."*.

### Stating Your Nationality
Nationalities in Portuguese are adjectives, meaning they change their ending to match your gender. If you are male, you use one ending; if you are female, you use another. 

Here are a few common nationalities:

*   **American**: americano (male) / americana (female)
*   **Canadian**: canadense (used for both male and female)
*   **British**: britânico (male) / britânica (female)
*   **Australian**: australiano (male) / australiana (female)

To state your nationality, you just add the word to your introduction:
*   *Eu sou americano.* (If you are a male from the US)
*   *Eu sou americana.* (If you are a female from the US)

Notice the pronunciation of the **a** in *americano* and *americana*. The first **a** is an **Open A:**. The final **o** in *americano* is a **Closed O:**, and the final **a** in *americana* is an **Open A:**. 

### Stating Your Origin
To say where you are from geographically, you will use the phrase:
*   **Eu sou de...**: I am from...

The word **de** means "from" or "of." The **e** here is a **Closed E:**. 

You can place the name of your country or city right after **de**:
*   *Eu sou de Londres.* (I am from London.)
*   *Eu sou de Nova Iorque.* (I am from New York.)
*   *Eu sou do Canadá.* (I am from Canada.)

Notice the last example uses **do** instead of **de**. In Portuguese, the word "from" sometimes combines with the word "the" (o) to form **do**. You do not need to memorize the grammar rules behind this right now—Chapter 3 will cover nouns and articles in depth. For now, just memorize the specific phrase for your home country or city.

### Putting It All Together
Let’s combine greeting, name, nationality, and origin into one smooth introduction. 

Imagine you are at a casual networking event in the evening:

> *Boa noite! Eu sou Sarah. Sou canadense e sou de Toronto.* 
> (Good evening! I am Sarah. I am Canadian and I am from Toronto.)

By mastering these three small building blocks—*"Eu sou [Name]", "Sou [Nationality]",* and *"Sou de [City/Country]"*—you can confidently introduce yourself to any Portuguese speaker.

## Basic Polite Expressions

Politeness is highly valued in Portuguese-speaking cultures. Taking the extra second to say "please," "thank you," and "excuse me" will immediately make your interactions warmer and more respectful. 

### Please
The word for "please" is:
*   **Por favor**

You can place *por favor* at the beginning or the end of a sentence. 
*   *Um café, por favor.* (A coffee, please.)
*   *Por favor, onde é o banheiro?* (Please, where is the bathroom?)

The **o** in *por* is an **Open O:**. The **o** in *favor* is also an **Open O:**.

### Thank You
The way you say "thank you" depends on your own gender:

*   **Obrigado**: Thank you (said by males)
*   **Obrigada**: Thank you (said by females)

This is a very important rule in Portuguese. If you are a man, you must say *obrigado*. If you are a woman, you must say *obrigada*. The word literally translates to "obliged," so it must agree with the gender of the person speaking. 

The first **o** in both words is an **Open O:**. The final **o** in *obrigado* is a **Closed O:**, and the final **a** in *obrigada* is an **Open A:**.

If you want to say "Thank you very much," you simply add the word **muito** (very/much) to the front:
*   *Muito obrigado.* (Thank you very much - male speaker)
*   *Muito obrigada.* (Thank you very much - female speaker)

In *muito*, the **o** is a **Closed O:**, and the **u** is silent. It sounds like "moy-toh."

### You're Welcome
When someone says thank you to you, you can respond with:
*   **De nada**: You're welcome / It's nothing

The **e** in *de* is a **Closed E:**, and the **a** in *nada* is an **Open A:**.

### Excuse Me
There are two main ways to say "excuse me," depending on the situation:

1.  **Com licença**: Excuse me (to get past someone or enter a space)
2.  **Desculpe**: Excuse me / Sorry (to apologize or get someone's attention)

**Com licença** literally translates to "with license." You use it when you are trying to walk past someone in a crowded grocery store, or when you are entering an elevator and need people to make room. 
*   The **o** in *com* is a **Closed O:**, and the **m** causes **nasalization**. 
*   The **e** at the end of *licença* is a **Closed E:**. Remember from your pronunciation chapter that the **ç** has a **cedilla (,)** underneath it, which tells you to pronounce it like an "s" sound.

**Desculpe** is used if you bump into someone by accident, or if you need to stop someone on the street to ask for directions. The final **e** is a **Closed E:**.

## A Real-World Scenario

Let’s put all of these phrases together into a single, realistic interaction. 

**Setting:** You (a male traveler) arrive at a small hotel in Porto, Portugal, in the late afternoon. You approach the front desk clerk.

> **You:** *Boa tarde.* (Good afternoon.)
> **Clerk:** *Boa tarde! Posso ajudar?* (Good afternoon! Can I help?)
> **You:** *Sim, com licença.* (Yes, excuse me.) *Eu sou David. Sou americano e sou de Chicago.* (I am David. I am American and I am from Chicago.)
> **Clerk:** *Bem-vindo, David!* (Welcome, David!)
> **You:** *Um quarto, por favor.* (A room, please.)
> **Clerk:** *Sim, um momento.* (Yes, one moment.)
> **You:** *Muito obrigado.* (Thank you very much.)
> **Clerk:** *De nada.* (You're welcome.)

Even with only a handful of phrases, you have successfully navigated a polite, real-world exchange: greeted the clerk based on the time of day, excused yourself, introduced your name, nationality, and origin, made a polite request, and exchanged basic courtesies.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Time-based greetings are mandatory:** Use **Bom dia** (morning), **Boa tarde** (afternoon), and **Boa noite** (evening/night) instead of a generic "hello."
*   **Informal vs. Formal:** Use **Olá** or **Oi** for casual situations. Use **Senhor** or **Senhora** paired with a time-based greeting for formal situations.
*   **Simple Introductions:** You do not need complex grammar to introduce yourself. Use **Eu sou [Name]** (I am), **Sou [Nationality]** (I am [Nationality]), and **Sou de [Place]** (I am from [Place]).
*   **Gender matters for the speaker:** Men say **obrigado** and women say **obrigada**. This rule also applies to your nationality (e.g., *americano* vs. *americana*).
*   **Polite expressions open doors:** Memorize **por favor** (please), **de nada** (you're welcome), **com licença** (excuse me, to pass), and **desculpe** (excuse me/sorry, to apologize).
*   **Saying Goodbye:** Use **tchau** for everyday departures, **até logo** for "see you later," and **adeus** for formal or long-term goodbyes.

modules.title: Nouns, Articles, and Gender
modules.content:
## The World of Nouns

Imagine you are walking through a bustling market in Lisbon. You see an apple, a banana, and a strawberry. In English, you simply say "the apple," "a banana," and "the strawberry." The words "the" and "a" stay exactly the same regardless of what fruit you are pointing at. 

In Portuguese, however, the strawberry is masculine, the apple is feminine, and the banana is feminine. To say "the strawberry," you use *o morango*. To say "the apple," you switch to *a maçã*. The little word for "the" changes depending on the gender of the noun. 

A **noun** is a word that represents a person, place, thing, or idea. In Portuguese, every single noun has a **gender**. It is either **masculine** or **feminine**. This doesn't mean that a table is biologically female or that a car is biologically male; gender is simply a grammatical category that Portuguese uses to organize words. 

Because every noun has a gender, the words that accompany nouns—like "the" or "a"—must change to match. This matching process is called **agreement**. If a noun is feminine, its article must be feminine. If a noun is masculine, its article must be masculine. 

## Predicting Gender by Word Endings

While English nouns don't have grammatical gender, Portuguese nouns follow a fairly reliable set of rules based on their final letters. You can usually guess a noun's gender just by looking at how it ends.

### The General Rule: O vs. A
The most common way to determine a noun's gender is to look at its last vowel. 

*   **Masculine nouns** typically end in the letter **-o**.
*   **Feminine nouns** typically end in the letter **-a**.

Let's look at some examples. Remember from our work on pronunciation that the unaccented **-o** at the end of a word is pronounced as a **Closed O:** (sounding similar to the "u" in "put"), and the unaccented **-a** is an **Open A:** (sounding like the "a" in "father").

**Masculine (-o):**
*   *livro* (book)
*   *carro* (car)
*   *queijo* (cheese)

**Feminine (-a):**
*   *casa* (house)
*   *mesa* (table)
*   *janela* (window)

### Exceptions and Other Endings
Not all nouns end in -o or -a. Nouns ending in other letters are assigned a gender, too. Here is how to handle them:

*   **Ending in -ade, -gem, -ção:** These are almost always **feminine**. 
    *   *cidade* (city)
    *   *viagem* (trip/journey)
    *   *nação* (nation)
*   **Ending in -im, -om, -l, -z:** These are generally **masculine**.
    *   *jardim* (garden)
    *   *homem* (man) — Note: This is a rare exception where a noun ending in -em is masculine.
    *   *animal* (animal)
    *   *país* (country) — Remember, the **s s** pronunciation applies here, sounding like the "s" in "sun."
*   **Ending in -e:** Nouns ending in -e are a mixed bag. Some are masculine, and some are feminine. You will need to memorize their gender as you learn them.
    *   *restaurante* (restaurant) - masculine
    *   *garrafa* (bottle) - feminine (ends in -a, so it follows the general rule)
    *   *noite* (night) - feminine

### Nouns Ending in a Tilde
In our chapter on Foundations of Pronunciation, we explored the tilde (~) and how it creates a **nasal vowel** in words like *pão* (bread) and *mãe* (mother). Nouns ending in a tilde generally follow the gender of their base word or require memorization. 

*   *mãe* (mother) is feminine.
*   *pão* (bread) is masculine. 
*   *limão* (lemon) is masculine. (You might recall *limões* from our discussion of nasalization in plural forms).

When you encounter a noun with a tilde, take a moment to note its gender. The ending alone won't give you a perfect rule.

## Definite Articles: Saying "The"

A **definite article** is the grammatical term for the word "the." It is used to refer to a specific, identified item. In English, we have just one: *the*. 

In Portuguese, the definite article must match the noun in both gender (masculine or feminine) and number (singular or plural). We will start with the singular forms.

*   **O** is used for masculine singular nouns.
*   **A** is used for feminine singular nouns.

Let's see them in action:

*   *o livro* (the book)
*   *o carro* (the car)
*   *a casa* (the house)
*   *a mesa* (the table)

### A Pronunciation Note on Articles
When you say *o livro*, the **O** is an isolated letter. In Portuguese, the isolated letters **A, E, I, O, U** are pronounced with their open vowel sounds. So, the article **O** is pronounced with an **Open O:** (sounding like the "o" in "hot" or the "a" in "law"), and the article **A** is pronounced with an **Open A:**. 

## Indefinite Articles: Saying "A" or "An"

An **indefinite article** is the grammatical term for the words "a," "an," or "one." It is used to refer to a non-specific item. In English, we use "a" before consonants and "an" before vowels. 

In Portuguese, indefinite articles are much simpler. Like definite articles, they simply change to match the gender of the noun. They do not change based on the first letter of the following word.

*   **Um** is used for masculine singular nouns.
*   **Uma** is used for feminine singular nouns.

Let's look at some examples:

*   *um livro* (a book)
*   *um carro* (a car)
*   *uma casa* (a house)
*   *uma mesa* (a table)

Notice how the indefinite articles *um* and *uma* end in -m and -a, respectively, perfectly mirroring the gender rules we just learned for nouns.

## Making Nouns Plural: Standard Spelling Rules

So far, we have looked at singular nouns—one book, one house. But what happens when you have two books or three houses? 

Forming plurals in Portuguese is highly systematic. Instead of adding an "s" haphazardly, you apply spelling rules based on the final letter of the word. 

### Rule 1: Words ending in a Vowel
If a noun ends in a pronounced vowel (**A, E, I, O, U**), you simply add an **-s** to the end.

*   *o livro* → *os livros* (the book → the books)
*   *a casa* → *as casas* (the house → the houses)
*   *o pai* → *os pais* (the father → the fathers)
*   *a maçã* → *as maçãs* (the apple → the apples)

### Rule 2: Words ending in -m
If a noun ends in **-m**, you drop the -m and add **-ns**. 

*   *um homem* → *uns homens* (a man → some men)
*   *a viagem* → *as viagens* (the trip → the trips)

### Rule 3: Words ending in -ão
Words ending in **-ão** (with the tilde creating the nasal vowel sound) have three possible plural endings: -ãos, -ães, or -ões. There is no universal rule for which one to use, so you must memorize the plural form when you learn the word. 

*   *o pão* → *os pães* (the bread → the breads)
*   *o limão* → *os limões* (the lemon → the lemons)
*   *a mão* → *as mãos* (the hand → the hands)

### Rule 4: Words ending in -l
If a noun ends in **-l**, you drop the -l and add **-is**. 

*   *o animal* → *os animais* (the animal → the animals)
*   *o jornal* → *os jornais* (the newspaper → the newspapers)

### Rule 5: Words ending in -r or -z
If a noun ends in **-r** or **-z**, you add **-es**.

*   *o flor* → *os flores* (the flower → the flowers)
*   *a luz* → *as luzes* (the light → the lights)

## Plural Articles

Just as singular nouns need matching singular articles, plural nouns need matching plural articles. 

**Definite Articles (The):**
*   **Os** is used for masculine plural nouns.
*   **As** is used for feminine plural nouns.

**Indefinite Articles (Some):**
*   **Uns** is used for masculine plural nouns.
*   **Umas** is used for feminine plural nouns.

While the English indefinite articles "a" and "an" do not have a plural form (we usually say "some" or nothing at all, like "I have books"), Portuguese uses *uns* and *umas* to mean "some" or "a few."

Let's look at a full scenario to see how nouns and articles change together from singular to plural:

**Singular:**
*   *o carro* (the car) → *os carros* (the cars)
*   *a mesa* (the table) → *as mesas* (the tables)
*   *um limão* (a lemon) → *uns limões* (some lemons)
*   *uma viagem* (a trip) → *umas viagens* (some trips)

## A Note on Pronunciation Shifts in Plurals

When you add an -s to a noun, it often changes how the final vowel is pronounced. In our Foundations of Pronunciation chapter, we discussed how an unaccented -o at the end of a word is a **Closed O:**. When you make it plural, the -o generally opens up.

*   *o livro* (the **Closed O:** sound) → *os livros* (the **Open O:** sound)
*   *o jogo* (the game, **Closed O:**) → *os jogos* (the games, **Open O:**)

Similarly, an unaccented -e at the end of a word is often a **Closed E:**. When pluralized, it often becomes an **Open E:**.

*   *o restaurante* (the **Closed E:**) → *os restaurantes* (the **Open E:**)

This shift happens because the addition of the -s changes the stress and syllable structure of the final syllable, naturally opening the vowel. 

## Key Takeaways

*   Every Portuguese noun has a gender: **masculine** or **feminine**.
*   Masculine nouns typically end in **-o**, while feminine nouns typically end in **-a**. Nouns ending in -ade, -gem, or -ção are usually feminine. Nouns ending in -im, -om, -l, or -z are usually masculine.
*   **Definite articles** ("the") are *o* (masculine singular) and *a* (feminine singular). Their plural forms are *os* and *as*.
*   **Indefinite articles** ("a" or "an") are *um* (masculine singular) and *uma* (feminine singular). Their plural forms, meaning "some," are *uns* and *umas*.
*   Articles must always **agree** with the noun in both gender and number.
*   Plural nouns are formed by applying standard spelling rules based on the final letter: add -s for vowels, change -m to -ns, change -l to -is, add -es for -r/-z, and memorize the three variations for -ão words (-ãos, -ães, -ões).

modules.title: Subject Pronouns and the Verb 'To Be'
modules.content:
## The Two Ways to Exist: Why Portuguese Needs Two Verbs for "To Be"

Imagine you are at a café in Lisbon. A friend introduces you to a Brazilian traveler named João, and you want to tell him how happy you are to meet him. You also want to tell him that you are a teacher, and finally, mention that you are a little cold because the air conditioning is on. 

In English, you would use the exact same verb for all three statements: "I **am** happy," "I **am** a teacher," and "I **am** cold." 

Portuguese, however, draws a strict line through these experiences. Because João is permanently a teacher, but only temporarily happy and temporarily cold, Portuguese forces you to choose between two entirely different verbs to express "to be." Making this choice correctly is one of the most important early steps in sounding natural in Portuguese. Before we can learn how to use these two verbs, however, we first need to know exactly *who* is being or doing something. 

## Subject Pronouns: Who is Doing the Action?

A **subject pronoun** is a word that takes the place of a noun and indicates who or what is performing the action in a sentence. In English, our subject pronouns are *I, you, he, she, it, we,* and *they*. 

In Chapter 3, we learned that every Portuguese noun has a gender (masculine or feminine). Because Portuguese is highly gendered, its subject pronouns reflect this, often changing based on whether the speaker is male or female, or whether a group includes men or women.

Here are the Portuguese subject pronouns, broken down by who they represent:

### Singular Pronouns (Referring to one person)
*   **Eu** (I): Used regardless of your gender. 
*   **Tu** (You - informal): Used informally for "you" in some regions, particularly in Portugal and parts of Brazil. 
*   **Você** (You - formal/polite): Used as a polite "you" in Portugal and as the standard, everyday "you" across most of Brazil. 
*   **Ele** (He): Used for a male person.
*   **Ela** (She): Used for a female person.

### Plural Pronouns (Referring to multiple people)
*   **Nós** (We): Used regardless of the gender makeup of the group. 
*   **Vocês** (You all): The plural of *você*. Used to address a group of people. (Note: English doesn't have a distinct word for "you all," but *vocês* covers it).
*   **Eles** (They - masculine/mixed): Used for a group of males, or a mixed group containing at least one male. 
*   **Elas** (They - feminine): Used *only* for groups made up entirely of females.

### The Pronoun Portuguese Skips: "It"
You might have noticed a glaring omission: there is no Portuguese word for "it." In Portuguese, objects and animals are not gender-neutral "its." As we saw with nouns and articles in Chapter 3, everything is either masculine or feminine. If you want to say "It is open," you simply use *ele* (if the noun is masculine) or *ela* (if the noun is feminine). You will see exactly how this works in the scenarios below.

## The Verb "Ser": Expressing Identity and Permanence

The first of our two "to be" verbs is **ser**. We use *ser* to describe the fundamental nature of a person or thing. If a characteristic is expected to last a long time, or if it defines who or what the subject is, you use *ser*.

You use **ser** for:
*   **Nationality and origin:** Where someone is from.
*   **Profession:** What someone does for a living.
*   **Physical traits:** Permanent physical descriptions like height or hair color.
*   **Identity:** Names and relationships (e.g., "She is my sister").
*   **Inherent characteristics:** The fundamental nature of an object (e.g., "The sky is blue," "Water is wet").

Because verbs change their spelling to match the subject pronoun (a process called **conjugation**), we cannot just say "ser." We have to change the word depending on who we are talking about. 

Here is the present tense conjugation of **ser**:

*   **Eu sou** (I am)
*   **Tu és** (You are - informal singular)
*   **Você é** (You are - formal/polite singular)
*   **Ele / Ela é** (He / She / It is)
*   **Nós somos** (We are)
*   **Vocês são** (You all are)
*   **Eles / Elas são** (They are)

### A Scenario at the Café

Let’s look at how this works in practice. You are sitting with your new friend João. You want to tell him about yourself and your sister, Maria. 

If you are male, you would say:
> "**Eu sou** professor." (I am a teacher.)

If you are female, you would say:
> "**Eu sou** professora." (I am a teacher.)

Notice how the noun for the profession changes to match your gender, but the verb (*sou*) stays the same. 

Now, you introduce your sister Maria:
> "**Ela é** Maria. **Ela é** médica." (She is Maria. She is a doctor.)
> "**Nós somos** do Canadá." (We are from Canada.)

Finally, you point to the café door, which was covered in Chapter 3. Because the word for door (*porta*) is feminine, you use the feminine pronoun *ela* to represent "it":
> "**Ela é** aberta." (It [the door] is open.)

## The Verb "Estar": Expressing States and Locations

While *ser* handles the permanent facts of life, **estar** handles the temporary ones. If a condition is a passing state, an emotion, or a physical location, you use *estar*.

You use **estar** for:
*   **Emotions:** How you are feeling right now (happy, sad, tired, angry).
*   **Temporary physical states:** Being sick, being cold, being hungry.
*   **Location:** Where someone or something is physically located at this moment. (Note: In Portuguese, you are not "in" a room; you "are" a room. Location always uses *estar*).
*   **Temporary conditions:** The weather right now, or the temporary state of an object (e.g., "The door is currently closed").

Here is the present tense conjugation of **estar**. Notice that the verb stem changes slightly for some pronouns, and all forms require an accent mark on the final *á* (except *tu*).

*   **Eu estou** (I am)
*   **Tu estás** (You are - informal singular)
*   **Você está** (You are - formal/polite singular)
*   **Ele / Ela está** (He / She / It is)
*   **Nós estamos** (We are)
*   **Vocês estão** (You all are)
*   **Eles / Elas estão** (They are)

### Returning to the Café Scenario

Let’s go back to the café with João. Earlier, you used *ser* to say you are a teacher. Now, you want to describe how you are feeling in this exact moment. 

To say you are happy right now, you say:
> "**Eu estou** feliz." (I am happy.)

You want to mention that you are cold because of the air conditioning:
> "**Eu estou** com frio." (I am cold. *Literally: I am with cold.*)

Where is João right now? To express his location, you use *estar*:
> "**João está** no café." (João is at the café.)

And what about the café window? It is closed right now, blocking the street noise. Because *janela* (window) is a feminine noun, you use *ela* for "it":
> "**Ela está** fechada." (It [the window] is closed.)

Notice the difference: the door is permanently an "open" type of door (*ser*), but the window is only temporarily closed right now (*estar*).

## Ser vs. Estar: Making the Right Choice

The fastest way to master these two verbs is to contrast them directly using the exact same adjectives. Changing the verb completely alters the meaning of the sentence.

### The "Bored" vs. "Boring" Distinction
In English, we use different words to say "I am bored" versus "I am a boring person." In Portuguese, you use the exact same adjective, but you change the verb:

*   **Eu sou chato.** (I am boring. -> This is my personality.)
*   **Eu estou chato.** (I am bored / I am being annoying right now. -> This is a temporary mood.)

### The "Marriage" vs. "Dating" Distinction
*   **Eles são casados.** (They are married. -> Marriage is a permanent, legal state, so we use *ser*.)
*   **Eles estão juntos.** (They are together / dating. -> This is a current relationship status that could change, so we use *estar*.)

### The "Origin" vs. "Location" Distinction
This is a common trap for beginners. Where you are *from* is permanent; where you are *right now* is temporary.

*   **Eu sou de Portugal.** (I am from Portugal. -> Origin uses *ser*.)
*   **Eu estou em Portugal.** (I am in Portugal. -> Current physical location uses *estar*.)

## A Note on Pronunciation

Building on our foundations in Chapter 1, it is helpful to pay attention to a few pronunciation details now that we are stringing full sentences together:

*   **The Open and Closed O:** In the verb *estar*, the first 'o' in **estou** is a closed vowel, sounding very much like the "oo" in the English word "moon." However, in the word **nós** (we), the 'o' is an open vowel, sounding closer to the "aw" in "law." 
*   **Nasalization:** The pronoun **nós** contains a nasal vowel. The air should escape through your nose as you pronounce the 'o', blending smoothly into the 's' without a hard break.
*   **Word Linking:** Portuguese speakers rarely pause between words. If you say "Eu estou" (I am), it will sound much more like "Eweshtou" in natural speech. The 'u' at the end of "Eu" glides directly into the 'e' of "estou."

## Practice: Which Verb Would You Use?

Read the following scenarios. Before reading the answer, decide whether you would use **ser** or **estar**, and which pronoun you would use.

**1. You want to tell someone your name.**
*   *Answer:* **Ser**. Your name is a permanent identity. "Eu sou [Name]."

**2. You want to say that you and your friends are currently at the beach.**
*   *Answer:* **Estar**. Physical location is always temporary. "Nós estamos na praia."

**3. You want to say that your female friend is a doctor.**
*   *Answer:* **Ser**. A profession is a defining, long-term characteristic. "Ela é médica."

**4. You want to say that you are tired today.**
*   *Answer:* **Estar**. Being tired is a passing physical state. "Eu estou cansado" (if you are male) or "Eu estou cansada" (if you are female).

**5. You want to say that the masculine noun (*o carro* - the car) is broken down right now.**
*   *Answer:* **Estar**. The car is not permanently broken; it is a temporary condition. Because *carro* is masculine, you use *ele*. "Ele está quebrado."

## Key Takeaways

*   Portuguese uses **subject pronouns** (eu, tu, você, ele, ela, nós, vocês, eles, elas) to indicate who is performing the action. Pronouns reflect gender (ele/ela, eles/elas) and formality (tu/você).
*   There is no direct translation for the English pronoun "it." Instead, objects are referred to as **ele** (masculine) or **ela** (feminine) based on the gender of the noun.
*   The verb **ser** is used to express permanent characteristics, identities, professions, origins, and inherent traits.
*   The verb **estar** is used to express temporary states, emotions, physical conditions, and current physical locations.
*   Changing the verb changes the meaning of the sentence entirely: "Ele é feliz" means he is a happy person by nature, while "Ele está feliz" means he is happy right now.

modules.title: Regular Verbs in the Present Tense
modules.content:
## The Engine of Language: Verbs and Infinitives

Imagine you are sitting in a café in Lisbon. You want to tell your new Portuguese friend that you **speak** Portuguese, that you **drink** coffee, and that you **live** in the city. So far, you have learned how to introduce yourself and say how you are feeling using the verb "to be" (*ser* and *estar*). But to truly communicate your daily life, you need action words. You need verbs.

In Portuguese, verbs are the engine of your sentences. They tell us what is happening, who is doing it, and when. Before we learn how to make them run, we have to look at their default, unconjugated state. This is called the **infinitive**.

The infinitive is the raw, base form of a verb. In English, infinitives usually start with the word "to" (to speak, to drink, to live). In Portuguese, the infinitive is just one single word ending in either **-ar**, **-er**, or **-ir**. 

These three endings are crucial because they sort Portuguese verbs into three distinct families, known as **conjugation groups**:
*   **First Conjugation:** Verbs ending in **-AR** (e.g., *falar* - to speak)
*   **Second Conjugation:** Verbs ending in **-ER** (e.g., *beber* - to drink)
*   **Third Conjugation:** Verbs ending in **-IR** (e.g., *viver* - to live)

To use a verb in a sentence, you can't just use the infinitive. You have to **conjugate** it. Conjugation is the process of changing the verb's ending to match the person doing the action (I, you, he/she, we, they) and the time frame (present, past, future). 

In this chapter, we will focus entirely on the **present tense** to describe actions happening right now or habitual daily actions. Furthermore, we will look exclusively at **regular verbs**. Regular verbs are predictable; they follow exact, unchanging mathematical patterns based on their ending. Once you learn the pattern for an -AR verb, you can apply it to almost every other -AR verb you encounter.

## Conjugating Regular -AR Verbs

The -AR verbs are the most common type of verb in Portuguese. Let's use the verb **falar** (to speak) as our template. 

To conjugate a regular verb in the present tense, you follow a two-step process:
1. **Drop the infinitive ending** (-ar) to find the verb stem. For *falar*, the stem is **fal-**.
2. **Add the present tense ending** that matches the subject pronoun.

Recall the subject pronouns introduced in Chapter 4: *eu* (I), *tu* (you - informal, mostly Portugal), *ele/ela/você* (he/she/you-formal), *nós* (we), *eles/elas/vocês* (they/you-plural). 

Here is the conjugation chart for a regular -AR verb:

| Pronoun | Ending | Conjugation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eu** | -o | fal**o** | I speak |
| **Tu** | -as | fal**as** | You speak |
| **Ele/Ela/Você** | -a | fal**a** | He/She/You speak |
| **Nós** | -amos | fal**amos** | We speak |
| **Eles/Elas/Vocês** | -am | fal**am** | They/You speak |

### Pronunciation Notes for -AR Verbs

Building on the foundations of pronunciation from Chapter 1, pay close attention to the vowels in these endings:

*   The **-o** in *eu falo* features a **closed O:** sound, much like the "oo" in "boot" but shorter. 
*   The **-a** in *tu falas* and *ele fala* uses an **Open A:** sound, similar to the "a" in "father."
*   The **-amos** in *nós falamos* is pronounced with an open A: "fah-lah-moosh" in a Brazilian accent (where the final 's' sounds like 'sh'), or "fah-lah-moosh" / "fah-lah-muss" depending on the Portuguese regional accent.
*   The **-am** in *eles falam* is a nasalized sound. The "a" takes on a nasal quality, and the "m" is not fully pronounced. It sounds vaguely like "fah-lowng" but softer. 

### Common Regular -AR Verbs

Here are some highly useful regular -AR verbs you can conjugate using this exact same pattern:

*   **Trabalhar** (to work) -> *Eu trabalho* (I work)
*   **Estudar** (to study) -> *Ela estuda* (She studies)
*   **Comprar** (to buy) -> *Nós compramos* (We buy)
*   **Cantar** (to sing) -> *Eles cantam* (They sing)
*   **Dançar** (to dance) -> *Você dança* (You dance) *Note: When the verb stem ends in 'c' (like danç-), the 'c' gets a cedilla (ç) before the 'a' to maintain a soft 's' sound.*

### A Day in the Life: Using -AR Verbs

Let's look at a short scenario using these verbs. 

> O Ricardo trabalha na cidade. Ele estuda português. Eu e a Maria compramos café. Nós cantamos no carro!

Translation: Ricardo works in the city. He studies Portuguese. Maria and I buy coffee. We sing in the car!

## Conjugating Regular -ER Verbs

Now let's look at the second conjugation group: verbs ending in **-ER**. The process is exactly the same. Drop the -ER to find the stem, and add the new set of endings.

Let's use the verb **beber** (to drink). The stem is **beb-**.

| Pronoun | Ending | Conjugation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eu** | -o | beb**o** | I drink |
| **Tu** | -es | beb**es** | You drink |
| **Ele/Ela/Você** | -e | beb**e** | He/She/You drink |
| **Nós** | -emos | beb**emos** | We drink |
| **Eles/Elas/Vocês** | -em | beb**em** | They/You drink |

### Pronunciation Notes for -ER Verbs

*   The **-e** in *tu bebes* and *ele bebe* uses a **Closed E:** sound, similar to the "e" in "they" (but without the 'y' glide at the end). 
*   The **-em** in *eles bebem* is a nasal vowel. The "e" becomes nasalized, and the "m" is silent. It sounds similar to the nasal "an" in the French word "vin," or slightly like the end of the Portuguese word *pão* (bread), but based on an 'e' vowel sound. 

### Common Regular -ER Verbs

*   **Comer** (to eat) -> *Eu como* (I eat)
*   **Viver** (to live) -> *Ela vive* (She lives)
*   **Aprender** (to learn) -> *Nós aprendemos* (We learn)
*   **Escrever** (to write) -> *Eles escrevem* (They write)

## Conjugating Regular -IR Verbs

Finally, we have the third conjugation group: verbs ending in **-IR**. You will notice that the endings for -IR verbs are very similar to -ER verbs, with one crucial exception in the first person ("I").

Let's use the verb **viver**... wait, let's use **abrir** (to open) to avoid confusion. The stem is **abr-**.

| Pronoun | Ending | Conjugation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eu** | -o | abr**o** | I open |
| **Tu** | -es | abr**es** | You open |
| **Ele/Ela/Você** | -e | abr**e** | He/She/You open |
| **Nós** | -imos | abr**imos** | We open |
| **Eles/Elas/Vocês** | -em | abr**em** | They/You open |

Notice the differences:
1. For **Eu** (I), -ER and -IR verbs share the same ending: **-o**.
2. For **Tu, Ele/Ela/Você, and Eles/Elas/Vocês**, -ER and -IR verbs share the exact same endings (-es, -e, -em).
3. The only difference between -ER and -IR verbs in the present tense is the **Nós** form: -ER verbs use **-emos**, while -IR verbs use **-imos**. 

### Pronunciation Notes for -IR Verbs

*   The **-o** in *eu abro* is a **closed O:** sound.
*   The **-e** in *ele abre* is a **Closed E:** sound.
*   The **-i** in *nós abrimos* features a clear "ee" sound, like the "i" in "machine."
*   The **-em** in *eles abrem* is the exact same nasal vowel sound as in *eles bebem*.

### Common Regular -IR Verbs

*   **Assistir** (to watch/attend) -> *Eu assisto* (I watch)
*   **Decidir** (to decide) -> *Ela decide* (She decides)
*   **Discutir** (to discuss) -> *Nós discutimos* (We discuss)
*   **Partir** (to leave/depart) -> *Eles partem* (They leave)

## Building Conversational Sentences

Knowing how to conjugate verbs is only half the battle; you need to plug them into basic sentence structures. In Portuguese, the subject pronoun (I, you, he, she) is often dropped because the verb ending already tells you who is doing the action. For example, saying *falo português* is perfectly clear and translates to "I speak Portuguese." However, as a beginner, including the pronoun helps reinforce the habit and avoids ambiguity.

Here is a practical scenario putting all three conjugation groups together. 

**Scenario:** You are at a language exchange meetup. You want to explain your daily routine to a new acquaintance.

*   "Eu **comero** café da manhã." (Wait, *comer* is to eat. Let's fix the conjugation.) -> "Eu **como** café da manhã." (I eat breakfast.)
*   "Depois, eu **bebo** água." (Afterward, I drink water.)
*   "Eu **estudo** português." (I study Portuguese.)
*   "À tarde, eu **trabalho**." (In the afternoon, I work.)
*   "À noite, eu **assistoo** televisão." (Wait, *assistir* is an -IR verb.) -> "À noite, eu **assisto** televisão." (At night, I watch television.)

### Connecting Nouns to Verbs

Remember from Chapter 3 (Nouns, Articles, and Gender) that Portuguese nouns are either masculine or feminine, and they require definite or indefinite articles. When your verb takes a direct object (the thing receiving the action), you must use the correct article.

Let's combine Chapter 3 and Chapter 5:

*   *Comer* (to eat) + *o bolo* (the cake - masculine) -> Eu **como o bolo**. (I eat the cake.)
*   *Beber* (to drink) + *a água* (the water - feminine) -> Ela **bebe a água**. (She drinks the water.)
*   *Comprar* (to buy) + *um carro* (a car - masculine) -> Nós **compramos um carro**. (We buy a car.)
*   *Abrir* (to open) + *a porta* (the door - feminine) -> Ele **abre a porta**. (He opens the door.)

Notice how the verb acts as the central pivot of the sentence, connecting the person doing the action to the gendered noun receiving it.

## Spotting the Patterns: A Quick Reference

The beauty of regular verbs is that you don't need to memorize every single verb individually. You just need to memorize the three sets of endings. Let's look at them side-by-side using our three model verbs: *falar*, *beber*, and *abrir*.

| Pronoun | -AR (Falar) | -ER (Beber) | -IR (Abrir) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eu** | fal**o** | beb**o** | abr**o** |
| **Tu** | fal**as** | beb**es** | abr**es** |
| **Ele/Ela/Você** | fal**a** | beb**e** | abr**e** |
| **Nós** | fal**amos** | beb**emos** | abr**imos** |
| **Eles/Elas/Vocês** | fal**am** | beb**em** | abr**em** |

**A memory trick for the "They/You all" form:** Notice that for -ER and -IR verbs, the *eles/elas/vocês* form ends in **-em**. The "m" at the end of a word in Portuguese is a signal to nasalize the preceding vowel. It is not a hard "m" sound like in the English word "man." 

**A memory trick for the "He/She/You" form:** For -ER and -IR verbs, the *ele/ela/você* form ends in just a single vowel: **-e**. It is incredibly common for beginners to accidentally add an "s" to the end of this (saying *ele bebes* instead of *ele bebe*). Remember that the "s" only belongs to the *tu* (you-informal) form.

## Key Takeaways

*   The **infinitive** is the base form of a verb. In Portuguese, infinitives always end in **-ar**, **-er**, or **-ir**. These represent the three conjugation groups.
*   **Conjugation** is the process of dropping the infinitive ending and adding a new ending to match the subject pronoun (who is doing the action).
*   For regular **-AR verbs** (like *falar*, *trabalhar*), the present tense endings are: **-o, -as, -a, -amos, -am**.
*   For regular **-ER verbs** (like *beber*, *comer*), the present tense endings are: **-o, -es, -e, -emos, -em**.
*   For regular **-IR verbs** (like *abrir*, *decidir*), the present tense endings are: **-o, -es, -e, -imos, -em**. The only difference between -ER and -IR verbs in the present tense is the **nós** form.
*   When speaking, pay attention to vowel sounds: the "o" in *eu falo* is a closed O:, while the "am" and "em" in the plural forms (like *eles falam* and *eles bebem*) require nasalization, similar to the nasal vowel in *pão*.

modules.title: Numbers, Days, and Time
modules.content:
## Counting from Zero to One Hundred

Imagine you are at a bustling farmers' market in Lisbon. You want to buy a dozen pastéis de nata, the vendor tells you the price, and you need to make sure you have the right amount of euros in your wallet. To navigate moments like this, you need numbers. 

In Portuguese, counting from 0 to 100 follows a highly logical pattern. Once you memorize the first nineteen numbers, the rest of the decade falls into place with just a few new vocabulary words.

### The First Nineteen

Let us start with the basics. Pay close attention to the pronunciation, especially the **nasal vowels** and the **open** and **closed** vowel sounds we explored in *Foundations of Pronunciation*.

*   **0** – zero
*   **1** – um (for masculine nouns) / uma (for feminine nouns)
*   **2** – dois (masculine) / duas (feminine)
*   **3** – três
*   **4** – quatro
*   **5** – cinco
*   **6** – seis
*   **7** – sete
*   **8** – oito
*   **9** – nove
*   **10** – dez
*   **11** – onze
*   **12** – doze
*   **13** – treze
*   **14** – quatorze (or catorze)
*   **15** – quinze
*   **16** – dezesseis
*   **17** – dezessete
*   **18** – dezoito
*   **19** – dezenove

Notice the numbers 16 through 19. They are essentially a combination of **dez** (ten) and the numbers six through nine: *dez-e-seis* (ten and six). 

Also, remember from *Nouns, Articles, and Gender* that Portuguese nouns are either masculine or feminine. The numbers one and two have to match the gender of the noun they describe. 
*   *Um carro* (One car - masculine)
*   *Uma casa* (One house - feminine)
*   *Dois gatos* (Two cats - masculine)
*   *Duas mesas* (Two tables - feminine)

### The Tens

To count by tens, you need to learn a new set of words. Some of these sound similar to their English equivalents, which will help you remember them.

*   **20** – vinte
*   **30** – trinta
*   **40** – quarenta
*   **50** – cinquenta
*   **60** – sessenta
*   **70** – setenta
*   **80** – oitenta
*   **90** – noventa
*   **100** – cem

### Combining Tens and Units

To say a number like 23 or 45, you say the tens, followed by the letter **e** (meaning "and"), followed by the unit. 

*   **23** – vinte e três (twenty and three)
*   **45** – quarenta e cinco (forty and five)
*   **68** – sessenta e oito (sixty and eight)
*   **92** – noventa e dois (ninety and two)

The only slight exception is 100. While 100 on its own is **cem**, if you add any numbers after it, it changes to **cento**.
*   100 – cem
*   101 – cento e um
*   125 – cento e vinte e cinco

### Larger Number Patterns

Once you hit 100, the pattern simply repeats itself. 
*   **200** – duzentos (masculine) / duzentas (feminine)
*   **300** – trezentos / trezentas
*   **400** – quatrocentos / quatrocentas
*   **500** – quinhentos / quinhentas
*   **1,000** – mil

Unlike English, which says "one thousand," Portuguese simply says **mil** for 1,000. To say 2,000, you say *dois mil* (two thousand). 

Because the hundreds (200 and above) act as adjectives describing a noun, they must also match the noun's gender. 
*   *Duzentos reais* (Two hundred reais - masculine currency)
*   *Duzentas pessoas* (Two hundred people - the word *pessoa* is feminine)

## The Calendar: Days, Months, and Seasons

Now that you can count, you can apply those numbers to the calendar. Scheduling a meeting, booking a flight, or making a dinner reservation all require knowing the days of the week, the months of the year, and the seasons.

### Days of the Week

In English, days of the week are capitalized. In Portuguese, they are not. Let us look at the seven days:

*   **Monday** – segunda-feira
*   **Tuesday** – terça-feira
*   **Wednesday** – quarta-feira
*   **Thursday** – quinta-feira
*   **Friday** – sexta-feira
*   **Saturday** – sábado
*   **Sunday** – domingo

You might notice a pattern here. The weekdays from Monday to Friday end in **-feira**. Historically, this comes from the Catholic tradition of naming the "fair days" leading up to Sunday. The words *segunda*, *terça*, *quarta*, *quinta*, and *sexta* literally translate to second, third, fourth, fifth, and sixth. 

Saturday (*sábado*) and Sunday (*domingo*) do not follow this pattern and stand alone. All days of the week are masculine nouns, so you say *o sábado* (the Saturday) or *a segunda-feira* (the Monday). 

### Months of the Year

Like the days of the week, months are not capitalized in Portuguese. The good news is that many of them sound very similar to English.

*   **January** – janeiro
*   **February** – fevereiro
*   **March** – março
*   **April** – abril
*   **May** – maio
*   **June** – junho
*   **July** – julho
*   **August** – agosto
*   **September** – setembro
*   **October** – outubro
*   **November** – novembro
*   **December** – dezembro

To say "in" a specific month, you use the preposition **em** combined with the month. 
*   *Em janeiro* (In January)
*   *Em dezembro* (In December)

### Seasons of the Year

Portugal and Brazil are located in different hemispheres, meaning their seasons are reversed. However, the vocabulary for the seasons remains the same. The word for season is **estação** (a feminine noun).

*   **Spring** – primavera
*   **Summer** – verão
*   **Autumn / Fall** – outono
*   **Winter** – inverno

To say "in the spring" or "in the winter," you combine the preposition **em** with the definite article **a** (for the feminine *primavera*) or **o** (for the masculine seasons).
*   *Na primavera* (In the spring — *em + a*)
*   *No verão* (In the summer — *em + o*)
*   *No outono* (In the fall)
*   *No inverno* (In the winter)

## Telling Time

Knowing the numbers and the calendar naturally leads us to telling time. To tell time in Portuguese, you will rely heavily on the verb **ser** (to be), which we covered in *Subject Pronouns and the Verb 'To Be'*. 

### Asking for the Time

To ask someone for the time, you say:
*   **Que horas são?** (What time is it?)

Notice that the word for "hours" is **horas**. Because *horas* is plural, the verb *ser* takes the plural form **são**. 

If you are asking specifically what time an event happens, you use the singular form:
*   **A que horas é o voo?** (At what time is the flight?)
*   **A que horas é a reunião?** (At what time is the meeting?)

### Stating the Time on the Hour

When answering, the structure depends on whether the hour is one o'clock or any other hour. 

For one o'clock, the verb *ser* is singular, and the word *hora* is singular:
*   **É uma hora.** (It is one o'clock.)

For all other hours, the verb is plural, and the word *horas* is plural:
*   **São duas horas.** (It is two o'clock.)
*   **São três horas.** (It is three o'clock.)
*   **São dez horas.** (It is ten o'clock.)

### Telling Time with Minutes

When you need to be precise, you use the word **e** (and) to add the minutes. 

*   2:10 – São duas horas e dez.
*   4:25 – São quatro horas e vinte e cinco.
*   7:45 – São sete horas e quarenta e cinco.

For the half-hour, you can say **e trinta** (and thirty), but it is much more common to use the phrase **e meia** (and a half).
*   3:30 – São três horas e meia.

### Morning, Afternoon, Evening, and Night

To clarify whether a time is in the morning or the evening, Portuguese uses specific expressions. These are essential because the 12-hour clock is used predominantly in spoken Portuguese.

*   **da manhã** – in the morning
*   **da tarde** – in the afternoon
*   **da noite** – in the evening / at night

You attach these directly to the end of the time:
*   **São oito horas da manhã.** (It is eight in the morning.)
*   **É uma hora da tarde.** (It is one in the afternoon.)
*   **São nove horas da noite.** (It is nine at night.)

### A Real-World Scenario: Booking a Train

Let us put this all together. Imagine you are at a train station in Porto. You approach the ticket counter.

**You:** *Bom dia. A que horas é o comboio para Lisboa?* 
(Good morning. At what time is the train to Lisbon?)

**Agent:** *O comboio é às duas horas da tarde.*
(The train is at two in the afternoon.)

**You:** *E o bilhete?* 
(And the ticket?)

**Agent:** *São quinze euros.*
(It is fifteen euros.)

In this brief exchange, you can see how numbers and time naturally intersect. The agent uses **às** (a contraction of the preposition *a* and the plural article *as*) to say "at" a specific time: *às duas horas*. 

When stating the price of the ticket, the agent uses the verb *ser* in the plural (*são*) because fifteen euros is a plural amount: *São quinze euros*. 

## Key Takeaways

*   Portuguese numbers 16–19 are built by combining **dez** (ten) with the numbers six through nine (e.g., *dezesseis*).
*   The numbers 1 (*um/uma*) and 2 (*dois/duas) change to match the gender of the noun they describe.
*   To say numbers like 23 or 45, link the tens and the units with the letter **e** (and): *vinte e três*.
*   Days of the week and months of the year are not capitalized in Portuguese. 
*   Monday through Friday end in **-feira**, while Saturday and Sunday stand alone.
*   To ask the time, use **Que horas são?** To state the time, use **São...** for plural hours and **É...** for one o'clock (*É uma hora*).
*   Use **e meia** for the half-hour (3:30 = *três horas e meia*).
*   Clarify the time of day by adding **da manhã** (morning), **da tarde** (afternoon), or **da noite** (evening/night) to the end of the time.

modules.title: Asking Questions and Negation
modules.content:
## The Power of Information

Imagine you are standing in a busy train station in Lisbon. You look at the departure board, but the information is confusing. You need to find out where your train is, when it leaves, and why the gate hasn't opened yet. To get these answers, you need two fundamental tools: the ability to ask questions and the ability to express what is *not* happening (negation). 

In Portuguese, asking questions and making sentences negative is refreshingly straightforward. Unlike English, which often requires rearranging the sentence or adding auxiliary verbs like "do" or "does" (e.g., "Where *do* you work?"), Portuguese keeps the word order exactly the same. You simply state a fact and change your tone of voice, or drop a question word at the beginning.

## Forming Yes/No Questions

The simplest type of question is one that can be answered with a simple "yes" (*sim*) or "no" (*não*). In Portuguese, you have three primary ways to form these questions without changing the grammatical structure of the sentence at all.

### 1. Raising Intonation
Just like in English, the easiest way to turn a statement into a question is to raise the pitch of your voice at the end of the sentence. 

- **Statement:** Você fala português. (You speak Portuguese.)
- **Question:** Você fala português? (Do you speak Portuguese?)

When speaking, your voice will naturally rise on the final word, *português*. When writing, you simply add a question mark. 

### 2. Adding Question Tags
If you want to seek confirmation or expect the listener to agree with you, you can add a "tag" to the end of a statement. In English, we say "You are going, aren't you?" or "She works here, right?" 

In Portuguese, the most common tag words are **não é?** (isn't it? / aren't they?), **né?** (a contraction of *não é*, common in spoken Brazilian Portuguese), and **certo?** (right?).

- **Statement:** O banco abre às nove. (The bank opens at nine.)
- **Question:** O banco abre às nove, **não é?** (The bank opens at nine, right?)
- **Question:** O banco abre às nove, **certo?** (The bank opens at nine, right?)

### 3. Placing "NÃO" Before the Verb
A very common and uniquely Portuguese way to ask a yes/no question is to place the word **não** at the beginning of the sentence, right before the verb. While *não* literally means "no," in this context, it functions like "Don't..." or "Aren't...".

- **Statement:** Você trabalha amanhã. (You work tomorrow.)
- **Question:** **Não** trabalha amanhã? (Aren't you working tomorrow? / Don't you work tomorrow?)
- **Question:** **Não** vai chover hoje? (Isn't it going to rain today?)

*Note: You might wonder why we use "vai" (goes) to talk about rain. This is a structure for expressing the future, which we will cover in detail in Chapter 10. For now, focus on the placement of "não" at the start of the question.*

## Interrogative Words: Getting the Details

Yes/no questions are useful, but they don't help you find out *where* the train is or *when* it leaves. For those details, you need interrogative words. 

In Portuguese, you form an information question by placing the interrogative word at the very beginning of the sentence, followed by the verb and the rest of the sentence. The word order does not flip.

Let's look at the five essential question words.

### O que? (What?)
Use **o que** to ask about things, objects, or actions. 
- **O que** é isso? (What is that?)
- **O que** você come? (What do you eat?)

*Pronunciation Note:* As we covered in the Foundations of Pronunciation, the letter **e** at the end of a word like *que* is a closed vowel. It sounds similar to the "ee" in the English word "cheese," but shorter. 

### Onde? (Where?)
Use **onde** to ask about locations.
- **Onde** você mora? (Where do you live?)
- **Onde** fica o banheiro? (Where is the bathroom?)

*Note: "Fica" comes from the verb "ficar," which literally means "to stay" or "to remain." In Portuguese, it is the most common way to ask where a place is located ("Where does the bathroom stay?").*

### Quando? (When?)
Use **quando** to ask about time, days, and dates. Since you already learned numbers, days, and time in Chapter 6, you can start combining them here.
- **Quando** você viaja? (When do you travel?)
- **Quando** a loja fecha? (When does the store close?)

### Por que? (Why?)
Use **por que** (written as two separate words) to ask for reasons or causes.
- **Por que** você estuda português? (Why do you study Portuguese?)

To answer a "why" question, you use **porque** (written as one word), which means "because."
- **Porque** eu gosto do Brasil. (Because I like Brazil.)

### Quem? (Who?)
Use **quem** to ask about people.
- **Quem** é você? (Who are you?)
- **Quem** fala inglês aqui? (Who speaks English here?)

## The Rule of Negation

Making a sentence negative in Portuguese is wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about adding auxiliary verbs or changing the main verb's form. You simply place the word **não** immediately before the verb.

- **Affirmative:** Eu trabalho hoje. (I work today.)
- **Negative:** Eu **não** trabalho hoje. (I do not work today.)

- **Affirmative:** Ela come carne. (She eats meat.)
- **Negative:** Ela **não** come carne. (She does not eat meat.)

Notice that there is no equivalent to the English "do not" or "does not." The verb itself stays exactly the same. You just put *não* in front of it.

## Expanding Negation: Nunca (Never)

Sometimes "no" isn't strong enough, and you need to express that something *never* happens. To do this, you use the word **nunca** (never). 

Just like *não*, you place **nunca** immediately before the verb.

- Eu **nunca** como peixe. (I never eat fish.)
- Nós **nunca** viajamos de trem. (We never travel by train.)

**A Grammatical Quirk: Double Negatives**
In English, using a double negative (e.g., "I don't never eat fish") is considered grammatically incorrect. In Portuguese, it is perfectly acceptable and very common to use both *não* and *nunca* in the same sentence for emphasis. 

When you do this, *não* goes before the verb, and *nunca* goes after it.

- Eu **não** como **nunca** peixe. (I don't ever eat fish.)
- Ela **não** viaja **nunca** de trem. (She never travels by train.)

Both "Eu nunca como peixe" and "Eu não como nunca peixe" are grammatically correct. The version with the double negative just adds a bit more emphasis, implying "I absolutely never eat fish."

## Putting It All Together: A Scenario

Let's look at how questions and negation work together in a practical situation. 

Imagine you are at a small restaurant in Porto. You are trying to order lunch, but you have some dietary restrictions and need information.

**Waiter:** Boa tarde! O que você quer comer? 
*(Good afternoon! What do you want to eat?)*
